serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of goat milk is approximately one glass (240 ml / 244 g), commonly consumed as a beverage at breakfast or as part of a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, goat, fluid, whole — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171278)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole fluid goat milk used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre are consistent with USDA data and are preserved from the input record. USDA data used to supplement saturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 (low) for whole goat milk is consistent with published values for whole cow's milk (GI ~27–34) and is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 244 g serving calculated as (31 × 4.3 × 2.44) / 100 = 3.25; the input value of 1.3 reflects a smaller reference portion (approximately 100 ml). GL per 100 g portion = (31 × 4.3) / 100 = 1.33, rounded to 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (57 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium content (204 mg/100 g) supporting a beneficial sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Whole goat milk is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions; its naturally low sodium and meaningful potassium content support blood pressure management, though full-fat varieties contribute saturated fat (1.85 g/100 g) and low-fat options are preferable for those also managing cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 31) with moderate carbohydrate content (4.3 g/100 g as naturally occurring lactose) and no added sugar; protein (2.91 g/100 g) slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole goat milk is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (up to 240 ml per serving); its low GI and absence of added sugar make it a preferable dairy choice, but total carbohydrate intake from lactose should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget, and sweetened or flavoured goat milk products must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 1.85 g/100 g contributes to daily saturated fat intake; however, goat milk contains medium-chain fatty acids (caprylic and capric acid) which are metabolised differently from long-chain saturated fats and may have a less atherogenic profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include whole goat milk in moderation as part of a heart-healthy diet; low-fat or reduced-fat goat milk is preferred to limit saturated fat intake, and total dairy fat consumption should be monitored within the context of overall dietary fat targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (134 mg/100 g) and protein (2.91 g/100 g) support bone protection critical for RA patients on corticosteroids; medium-chain fatty acids may have mild anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Goat milk is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and provides calcium and protein to support bone health; fortified varieties are preferred to ensure adequate vitamin D intake, and individuals with dairy sensitivity should monitor their individual response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goat milk is the whole, unprocessed fluid milk obtained from domestic goats (Capra aegagrus hircus), one of the oldest and most widely consumed animal milks in the world. It is produced by milking lactating does and is consumed either raw (where legally permitted) or more commonly after pasteurisation to ensure microbiological safety. Unlike cow's milk, goat milk is not homogenised in many traditional preparations, meaning the cream rises naturally to the surface. The flavour of whole goat milk is mildly sweet with a characteristic slightly tangy, earthy note that becomes more pronounced in unpasteurised or aged forms; the texture is creamy and smooth, with a mouthfeel that many consumers describe as richer and more full-bodied than standard cow's milk despite a comparable fat content. The fat globules in goat milk are naturally smaller than those in cow's milk, and the protein matrix — dominated by alpha-s2 casein rather than alpha-s1 casein — forms a softer, more friable curd during digestion, which is widely cited as a reason why some individuals with mild cow's milk intolerance find goat milk easier to tolerate. Nutritionally, per 100 g, whole goat milk provides 57 kcal of energy, 2.91 g of protein, 2.97 g of total fat (of which 1.85 g is saturated fat, including the medium-chain fatty acids caprylic acid and capric acid), and 4.3 g of carbohydrate entirely as naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar. It is a meaningful dietary source of calcium (134 mg/100 g, approximately 13% of the adult daily reference intake), potassium (204 mg/100 g), phosphorus (111 mg/100 g), riboflavin (0.138 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (57 mcg RAE/100 g). Its glycemic index is low at 31, placing it firmly in the low-GI category, and the glycemic load per 100 g reference portion is approximately 1.3, making it a suitable dairy beverage for blood glucose management. Goat milk is consumed globally as a standalone beverage and serves as the base for a wide range of dairy products including goat cheese (chèvre, feta-style, aged varieties), goat yogurt, goat butter, and goat milk powder. It is particularly prominent in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and sub-Saharan African food cultures, where goat husbandry has historically been more prevalent than cattle farming due to the goat's adaptability to arid and mountainous terrain. Clinically, goat milk is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension (low sodium at 57 mg/100 g, beneficial potassium-to-sodium ratio), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI of 31, no added sugar, protein-mediated attenuation of postprandial glucose rise), and rheumatoid arthritis (calcium and protein for bone protection in patients on corticosteroids). For cardiovascular disease, moderate consumption is acceptable but low-fat variants are preferred to limit saturated fat intake. Goat milk is not suitable as a substitute for breast milk or infant formula, and individuals with a confirmed cow's milk protein allergy should exercise caution as cross-reactivity with goat milk proteins is possible. It is naturally gluten-free and halal, and is suitable for lacto-vegetarians.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171278) for whole fluid goat milk. Micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI value of 31 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for whole milk. All values are internally consistent and supported by two independent approved sources.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central — milk contains no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant milk (vaccenic acid) are present at trace levels below reporting threshold; set to 0.0 as per USDA SR Legacy reporting. calcium_mg in the input per_100g was reported as 0.0 by HPB; this has been corrected to 134.0 mg/100 g per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171278), as a calcium value of zero for whole goat milk is inconsistent with all known approved sources and is likely a data entry omission. iron_mg in the input per_100g was reported as 0.0 by HPB; USDA SR Legacy reports 0.05 mg/100 g, which is a confirmed low but non-zero value; updated accordingly. The input health_suitability entry for diabetes incorrectly classified goat milk as 'suitable: false' and included an erroneous caution referencing a 'sweetened instant drink mix' which is not applicable to plain whole goat milk; this has been corrected. The input dietary_tags included 'wholegrain' and 'keto' which are not appropriate for whole goat milk; these have been removed. Potassium value of 204 mg/100 g sourced from USDA SR Legacy as HPB did not report this nutrient.
